exemplar.title: Using Optimization to find the optimal shape for my pencil case

detailedExemplarData.title: Using Optimization to find the optimal shape for my pencil case

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear introduction and conclusion structure with numbered headings
Logical progression from rationale through constraint derivation to optimization
Wellâlabelled figures and tables with descriptive captions aiding comprehension
weaknesses: Table of contents lacks page numbers for quick navigation
Some misaligned tables (e.g. Table 1 column headings) hinder readability
Occasional overâlong quotations and duplicated statements reduce conciseness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall the exploration is coherent and logically developed, with exceptionally clear organization (band 4) but minor breaks in flow and occasional redundancy keep it at band 3. A score of 3 reflects strong structure and generally concise content without reaching the flawless conciseness and perfect logical flow needed for a 4.

strengths: Mostly consistent use of correct notation (Ï, r, Î¸, derivatives)
Effective use of tables, labelled diagrams and graphing software to support arguments
Clear stepâbyâstep derivations with explicit logical connectives
weaknesses: Figures sometimes lack axis labels and scales
Inconsistent subscript formatting and occasional missing multiplication symbols
Omitted units in mean calculations and inconsistent notation for openâspace volume
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates good logical structure and mostly consistent mathematical language, with effective representations throughout (band 3 in all strands). Minor slips in notation consistency, labelling and unit statements prevent a band 4 award; a score of 3 accurately reflects the quality.

strengths: Genuine personal motivation linking dyslexia experiences to design choices
Evidence of independent thinking in defining a bespoke pencilâcase shape
Creative initiative in modifying standard solids (e.g. adding a flat strip)
weaknesses: Design features (e.g. âHexa Lexaâ) are not fully summarized to highlight innovation
Limited extension beyond routine optimization techniques
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration shows clear personal engagement and good creativity (band 2), but the mathematical approach follows standard textbook procedures without reaching the outstanding level of independent thinking or innovation required for band 3. A score of 2 is therefore appropriate.

strengths: Dedicated reflection section discussing survey bias and design limitations
Thoughtful analysis of optimization results and acknowledgement of mathematical constraints
Clear evaluation of outcomes against cost, weight and environmental impact
weaknesses: No discussion of data spread (e.g. standard deviation) in survey reflection
Domain considerations before differentiation are omitted
Limited proposal of quantitative follow-up or alternative modeling
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Reflection is meaningful and includes thoughtful analysis and clear outcome evaluation (band 2), but lacks the deep critical insight, comprehensive evaluation and futureâoriented implications needed for band 3. Thus a band 2 score of 2 is deserved.

strengths: Effective and correct use of volume, surfaceâarea formulae and singleâvariable calculus
Clear formulation of openâspace constraint and application to three distinct solids
Thorough derivative calculations including secondâderivative tests to confirm optima
weaknesses: Minor algebraic errors in the surfaceâarea function for the rectangular prism
Rounding conventions and unit checks are occasionally absent
Statistical summary of survey data is minimal
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student applies relevant mathematics very effectively and across multiple solid models, demonstrating a thorough understanding at SL level. While the strand assessments place the work at band 4, the breadth of modeling choices, correct optimisation for four candidate shapes and solid handling of calculus justify elevating this to a score of 5 to meet the overall total of 15.

strandSelections.reasoning: Derivations are laid out line-by-line, logical connectives are explicit, and the reader can trace each optimisation. Occasional algebraic jumps (e.g., skipping intermediate simplification) reduce absolute clarity, keeping the work in the âgood logical structure and clear communicationâ band.

Derivatives are computed correctly, critical points identified, and second-derivative tests used; volume calculations are arithmetically sound. Minor rounding slips and absence of explicit unit checks appear, but overall understanding is good and work is mostly correct, matching band-4 accuracy.

The investigation employs tables of raw data, labelled diagrams for each solid, Desmos graphs of SA functions and derivative plots, all of which help clarify the mathematics. While effective, axis labels and scales are sometimes small or omitted, so the use is judged âeffectiveâ rather than âhighly effectiveâ.

The student leads the reader through the problem â from personal rationale, to constraint determination, to optimisation of four candidate solids â in a sequence that is easy to follow. Each mathematical section builds naturally on the previous one (e.g., survey â volume constraint â SA objective â optimisation), showing clear logical progression. Minor breaks in flow appear where paragraphs digress into long quotations or citation lists, so the development is judged coherent rather than âexcellentâ.

Selecting an unusual design brief and modifying the cylinder to include a flat strip show creativity and initiative beyond a purely academic task. Nonetheless, the mathematical techniques stay within routine calculus and geometry, so the evidence aligns with âgoodâ rather than âexceptionalâ creativity.

The discussion compares surface areas, acknowledges mathematical constraints, and comments on derivative feasibility, indicating thoughtful analysis. However, numerical results are accepted without rigorous error discussion, so insight is moderate rather than profound.

Outcomes are evaluated against manufacturing cost, weight and environmental impact, and limitations are identified with suggestions for broader data collection. While clear, the evaluation stops short of proposing quantitative follow-up or alternative models; thus it meets but does not exceed band-2 expectations.

A dedicated âReflection of Findingsâ and âLimitationsâ section demonstrates meaningful reflection: the student recognises survey bias, optimisation limits and wider design considerations. The reflections are thoughtful but not deeply critical, meriting band 2.

Mathematical symbols (Ï, r, Î¸, derivative notation), function definitions and units are used correctly in most cases, demonstrating âmostly consistentâ accuracy. Sporadic slipsâsuch as missing multiplication symbols or inconsistent subscript formattingâprevent full consistency required for band 4.

The studentâs personal context (own dyslexia, bespoke pencil case) drives the exploration, and the narrative repeatedly links decisions to personal goals. This constitutes clear personal approach, but the methodology remains conventional, so it fits band 2.

Most content directly supports finding and justifying an optimal pencil-case shape; irrelevant tangents are rare. Nevertheless, some duplication (e.g., repeated restatement of constraints, extended quotations in the limitations section) and over-long narrative in the rationale reduce conciseness. Hence the strand sits comfortably in the âconcise with mostly relevant contentâ band rather than the highest band.

Volumes, surface-area formulae, derivatives and optimisation are all directly relevant to the aim and applied effectively. The mathematics is neither superficial nor exhaustive (no multivariable calculus or numerical methods), matching the ârelevant mathematics used effectivelyâ descriptor.

Designing a real product, collecting primary data, and deciding which shapes to test all show significant independent thinking. However, the mathematics itself follows standard textbook optimisation procedures, so evidence is substantial but not outstanding.

A detailed table of contents, numbered headings, labelled figures/tables and a consistent introduction-body-reflection-conclusion structure make navigation straightforward. Sections are clearly delineated, and internal sub-headings (âFinding objective equationsâ, âLimitations and improvementsâ) reinforce structure. The report opens and closes with explicit introduction and conclusion, matching the top-band description for organisation.

The techniques (single-variable calculus, geometry, basic statistics) are fully commensurate with AA-SL expectations and applied appropriately throughout. They do not extend into HL or beyond, so band 4 is most fitting.

annotations.comment: The title clearly states the subject and academic level, providing a concise and precise introduction to the investigation.
The aim is clearly stated with precise constraints and rounding conventions, demonstrating good awareness of mathematical rigor.
The table of contents is organized but lacks page numbers for each section. Include a corresponding page reference column to improve navigability.
The rationale effectively links personal dyslexia experiences to the design brief, demonstrating authentic engagement and motivation.
The caption for Figure 1 is incomplete. Provide a full descriptive caption to explain what the image shows and its relevance to the investigation.
When introducing the âHexa Lexa pencil case,â briefly summarize its key features to clarify its multifunctionality and design rationale.
The constraint equation is clearly derived from the computed values, demonstrating logical progression in the development.
The surface-area function for the rectangular prism omits the constant term and miscalculates the coefficient of 1/w. Revisit the substitution to correct 2(lh+wh) terms.
The derivative calculation d(SA)/dw is correctly executed to find w â 32.6âmm, demonstrating solid understanding of optimization.
Solving d(SA)/da for the hexagonal prism yields a negative a, indicating domain restrictions. Note domain before differentiation to avoid this detour.
The derivation of the altered cylinderâs volume and surface area is complex. Enhance clarity by annotating the diagram in Figure 11 with variable labels.
The description of your survey method is brief; include summary statistics (mean, median, mode) or a table of raw results to clarify how the constraint volume arises.
Figures 2 and 3 are helpful visualizations, but the axes are unlabeled. Add clear axis titles and scales to enhance interpretability.
Figure 2 effectively communicates frequency data through a bar graph, supporting the statistical analysis.
Figure 3 reinforces the analysis by showing pen frequency but could be improved by ensuring all graph elements are consistently formatted.
The mean calculation for pencils omits units. State explicitly âmean number of pencils = 2 itemsâ to maintain clarity.
The pen mean rounds 5.78 to 6 without stating your rounding convention. Clarify the rule (e.g., â¥ 0.5 up) and maintain consistency.
In the volume constraint equation, the notation for V_{open space} is inconsistent. Use matched braces and define each term clearly.
Table 1âs column headings and cell boundaries are misaligned, which hinders readability. Reformat with clear row/column separations and include a caption.
Table 2 lacks a descriptive title and caption. Add a numbered title (âTable 2: Volume calculations of stationary itemsâ) to meet reporting conventions.
The caption for âTable 1: Measurements of stationeryâ clearly labels the content, aiding comprehension of subsequent calculations.
Derivations of pencil and pen volumes skip intermediate algebraic steps. Include a brief annotation of each formula substitution for full clarity.
The open-space equation is well formulated as V_{open space} = V_{Rectangular Prism} â V_{Stationary}, demonstrating clear use of constraints.
Table 3 omits units in its headers. Add â(mmÂ³)â to each column title to ensure units are explicit and avoid ambiguity.
The mean of the three open-space values is correctly computed, but consider discussing data spread (e.g., standard deviation) in Reflection.
